cards.title: CPA Is Your North Star
cards.front: If you stare at only one number to judge a campaign, which one tells the true story?
cards.back: Make CPA your PRIMARY success metric — it accounts for the whole funnel. Depending on the funnel it becomes CPL (lead), CPR (registration), or CPS (sale). Choose based on how well you know your numbers and how fast you need ROI: CPL is fine if follow-up sequences earn ROI later; CPS if you need ROI immediately. Look at the wrong metric and you'll read the wrong story.
cards.example: CPL works if your email sequence monetizes leads later; pick CPS when cash is needed now.

cards.title: CTR/CPC/CPM Are Diagnostics, Not Goals
cards.front: Why shouldn't you celebrate a high CTR or panic over a high CPC?
cards.back: Treat CTR/CPC/CPM as SECONDARY, diagnostic metrics. Facebook's many ad formats (short copy, long copy, video) make them 'apples to oranges' (unlike Google AdWords). A short-copy ad may out-CTR a video, but the video's clicker is more qualified. Use CTR/CPC/CPM to troubleshoot WHERE the funnel breaks when you miss target CPA.
cards.example: High lead cost + low CTR + high CPC together signal creative that isn't resonating with its audience.

cards.title: The CTR Formula & What's 'Good'
cards.front: What counts as a strong link CTR, and how is it even calculated?
cards.back: CTR = clicks / impressions (e.g., 10 / 1,000 = 1%; 344 / 376,409 = 0.091%). A HIGH link CTR is > 1% as a general rule of thumb — that's Link CTR, not CTR (All).
cards.example: 344 clicks on 376,409 impressions = 0.091% — well below the 1% benchmark.

cards.title: CTR (All) Lies for Off-Facebook Traffic
cards.front: Your CTR looks great — but you're sending people OFF Facebook. What's the catch?
cards.back: CTR (All) counts reactions, comments, shares, and profile clicks — not just link clicks. For ads driving traffic OFF Facebook, use CTR (Link Click-Through Rate) instead, or you'll badly overstate how many people actually clicked through to your site.

cards.title: What a 'Low' CPC Looks Like
cards.front: How cheap should a click be before you call it good?
cards.back: Low CPC (consumer) = < $0.50 when targeting consumers. Low CPC (B2B) = <= $1 for B2B clicks. Use these as rough floors, not absolute pass/fail lines.

cards.title: Impressions vs. Reach (Don't Confuse Them)
cards.front: 1.4M impressions — does that mean 1.4M people saw your ad?
cards.back: No. Impressions = opportunities to see (can repeat for the same person); Reach = unique people. 1.4M impressions / 200,000 reach = an average of 7 exposures per person. 1.4M impressions does NOT equal 1.4M people — reach reports actual unique humans.

cards.title: Frequency Caps & the Ad-Fatigue Zone
cards.front: How many times can you show an ad before even a great one stops working?
cards.back: Newsfeed frequency caps: 4/day for Page fans, 2/day for non-fans (the Right Hand Column can be much higher). Ad-fatigue zone kicks in around frequency ~10+ — even excellent ads lose clicks as people tire of them.

cards.title: Read & Diagnose Results in Ads Manager
cards.front: Where do you start when you open a messy Ads Manager report?
cards.back:
Top-down workflow from overview to root cause:
- Open Ads Manager; review default Performance view at the CAMPAIGN level (Results + Cost per Result for your objective).
- Drill into the Ad Set level (default Performance view) to judge winners vs. losers.
- Customize columns to understand WHY — use the 'Performance and clicks' preset, or build a custom view (Conversions > Website) if you have custom conversions.
- Cross-reference several metrics together, never in isolation.
- Use the Breakdown menu to segment by age, gender, country, etc.
- Export to Excel (button next to Breakdown) for deeper sorting when volume is high.
cards.example: High lead cost + low CTR + high CPC signals creative that isn't resonating.

cards.title: Custom Columns for a Lead-Gen Conversion Campaign
cards.front: Which exact columns should you pin to track both leads AND sales in one view?
cards.back:
For a website conversion campaign measuring both leads and sales, include:
- No. of Leads (Results)
- Cost Per Lead (Cost Per Result)
- CTR (Link Clicks)
- Link Clicks
- CPC (Cost per Link Click)
- Number of Sales
- Sales Conversion Value

cards.title: Never Read One Metric in Isolation
cards.front: Why can a single great-looking metric still hide a failing ad?
cards.back: Always cross-reference several metrics together, never in isolation. Reading the wrong metric (focusing on CTR/CPC/CPM instead of CPA) can make you think ads are tanking or thriving when the truth is the opposite. Combinations tell the story: high lead cost + low CTR + high CPC signals creative that isn't resonating with the audience it's shown to.

cards.title: Don't Kill Your Most Expensive Leads
cards.front: The ad set with the priciest leads — pause it, right?
cards.back: Not so fast. Don't kill expensive leads without checking lead QUALITY. In the example, the ad set with the highest lead cost, lowest CTR, and highest CPC drove the MOST sales and revenue. Accept higher lead cost when downstream revenue justifies it — always check downstream sales/revenue before pausing.
cards.example: Highest lead cost + lowest CTR + highest CPC ad set produced the most sales and revenue.

cards.title: Fixing High CPAs by Pausing Ad Sets
cards.front: Your CPA is bleeding — what's the systematic way to cut losers?
cards.back:
Cut losers systematically:
1. Know your target CPA.
2. Sort your reporting view by the last seven days.
3. Sort ad sets by cost of conversion, greatest to least.
4. Pause every ad set converting at 2-3x your target CPA.
5. Catch zero-converters: sort ad sets by Amount Spent (greatest to least) and turn off any that has spent 2x your CPA with zero conversions.

cards.title: Zero Conversions ≠ Zero Effect
cards.front: A campaign got zero attributed conversions. Was it useless?
cards.back: Assuming a zero-conversion campaign had no effect is one of the costliest analysis mistakes. In the attribution example, Campaign #1 got zero attributed conversions but may still have INFLUENCED the other six — cross-channel/assist effects are real. (Caveat: this is different from a zero-converter that has burned 2x your CPA, which you should pause.)
cards.example: Home Depot in-store sales spiked with no trackable attribution.

cards.title: Attribution Auto-Flows Down the Hierarchy
cards.front: Who does the work of tying each sale back to the exact ad that drove it?
cards.back: Facebook auto-attributes each conversion down to the exact campaign > ad set > ad IF the pixel is set up and configured properly — the heavy lifting is done for you. Default attribution window is 1-day view / 28-day click, adjustable in column customization.

cards.title: Choosing the Conversion Window (1-Day vs 7-Day)
cards.front: 1-day or 7-day click attribution — which should you pick, and why test both?
cards.back: Choose deliberately: use a 1-DAY window to test whether an audience/creative converts quickly; use 7-DAY CLICK when you have time — the algorithm pulls more data and often yields better results over time. The authors PREFER 7-day but always TEST 1-day vs 7-day per campaign. Options: 1-day or 7-day, click-only or click-or-view.

cards.title: Sequenced Optimization: Image, Then Copy, Then Audience
cards.front: Should you test audiences and creative all at once? What's the right order?
cards.back:
Win the Image, Then the Copy, Then the Audience — so audience results aren't muddied by weak creative:
1. Create ONE ad set with 2-3 ads that differ ONLY by image.
2. Run a few days; identify the best image.
3. Duplicate the winner and split-test two ad COPY versions using that same winning image; find the best copy.
4. You now have optimal image + optimal copy — only NOW begin testing audience.
5. To save money, wait to see how the first ad set performs before building more.
Caveat: with multiple ads in one ad set, Facebook quickly throttles ads it judges weaker, which can muddy clean test data.

cards.title: Always A/B Test — The Winner Surprises You
cards.front: The ad you're proudest of — is it the one that'll win?
cards.back: Always A/B test creatives. The winner is often the one the marketer liked LEAST; what matters is whether it conveys the message, not internal polish. The 'F' grade beat the 'A'; black-and-white beat color. Let the data decide.
cards.example: An 'F'-graded ad and a black-and-white version each beat the polished favorites.

cards.title: Optimize Toward Marketplace Results, Not Craft
cards.front: Should you judge your ad by how polished it is or by something else?
cards.back: Optimize toward marketplace results, not craft quality. Judge work by real outcomes (opt-ins, sales, customer results), not internal polish. There's a thin line between success and failure — many great copywriters/marketers don't know how to win big on Facebook because they optimize for craft instead of outcomes.

cards.title: Override Optimization & Delivery When It Makes Sense
cards.front: Facebook's default optimization follows your objective — when should you overrule it?
cards.back: Optimization & delivery defaults to the campaign objective, and you should usually accept Facebook's recommended conversion optimization. But override it when it makes sense — example: on a retargeting ad with a 'video views' objective, optimize for IMPRESSIONS instead to maximize frequency in front of your retargeting pool.
cards.example: Retargeting + video-views objective → optimize for impressions to hammer frequency.

cards.title: Facebook the Hook: Mine Boosted/Blog Data for a Hidden Hook
cards.front: What if your best-converting hook is already hiding in your old account data?
cards.back:
Surface a hook the customer has already proven. Steps (verbatim):
1. Set lifetime filter.
2. Search campaign name: blog.
3. Sort columns by performance and clicks.
4. Sort link clicks from highest to lowest.
5. Pay attention to CTR, CPC, and Relevance Score.
6. Analyze your data!
Also search 'post' and 'blog' with time filter 'Lifetime.' Use link clicks as the driving metric (people click if the subject resonates); CTR, CPC, and Relevance Score are secondary.
cards.example: Wicked Reports: blog post 'When Is the Best Time to Send Your Emails?' kept surfacing with an incredibly low CPC — the data screamed demand for a hook never raised in onboarding.

cards.title: Ad-to-Landing-Page Congruency ('Ad Scent')
cards.front: Why might a great ad still convert poorly once people click?
cards.back: Ensure ad-to-landing-page congruency. Match the landing page headline, copy, and even color scheme to the ad's hook ('ad scent') for good conversions. A mismatch breaks the scent trail and tanks conversion.

cards.title: Measure Organic First — Ads Manager Hides It
cards.front: Where do viral winners hide that Ads Manager will never show you?
cards.back: Measure organically first (Dennis Yu). Ads Manager and Power Editor do NOT show organic data, so a viral post can be missed. Spend most of your monitoring time in the Page timeline and Insights tab where organic + paid stats appear together; spot winners before deciding what to boost.

cards.title: The 95/5 Rule: Test LOTS of Ads
cards.front: Why does swinging for the fences on ad count pay off so disproportionately?
cards.back: Online results skew ~95/5 (95% of results from the top 5%); Facebook is closer to 97/3-98/2 due to Likes/Shares compounding. Of 100 ads, #1 gets 18% of traffic and the top 10 get 55%. CTR spread: 10 ads → best ~3x worst; 100 ads → best ~10x worst. Keep swinging — small CTR gains bring disproportionate traffic.
cards.example: Of 100 ads, the #1 ad captures 18% of traffic; the top 10 capture 55%.

cards.title: Relevance Score: The 1-10 Quality Gauge
cards.front: What does Facebook's 1-10 Relevance Score actually measure and affect?
cards.back: Relevance Score is a 1-10 scale (10 best), based on expected positive vs. negative feedback. It impacts CPA and conversions — a higher score generally means cheaper, better-performing delivery. Watch it alongside CTR and CPC as a secondary diagnostic.

cards.title: Shares Are Algorithmic Rocket Fuel
cards.front: Why is a share worth so much more than a like?
cards.back: Maximize shareability for algorithmic reward: more shares = cheaper clicks, impressions, and views, more impressions, and higher ROI. 1 share ~ 13x a like, and shares multiply reach (reported stats undercount secondary effects). 'Aha moment' content drives shares; shares also prime visitors' frame of mind on the landing page.
cards.example: Keto long-copy ad: 469,000 reach, 1,100 likes, 557 shares.

cards.title: Like-to-Share Ratios & 'Unicorn' Engagement
cards.front: How do you tell a genuinely viral post from a merely popular one?
cards.back: Like-to-Share ratio: consumer 2:1 is doing really well; B2B 3:1 is really good. 'Unicorn' engagement rate is > 10%. Example unicorn: 2,901 reactions / 16,384 reach = 18% (strong unicorn).
cards.example: 2,901 reactions / 16,384 reach = 18% engagement — a strong unicorn.

cards.title: How Many Conversions the Algorithm Needs
cards.front: How many conversions must you feed Facebook before its algorithm gets smart?
cards.back: Minimum conversions per ad set: 20-30/week (bare minimum) for large audiences — the algorithm needs volume. Preferred conversion volume: 10-20+ conversions/DAY fed to Facebook for optimal algorithm learning. Below the minimum, optimization data is too thin to trust.

cards.title: Audience Sweet Spots for Scaling (Michigan Method)
cards.front: How big should an audience be to give the algorithm room to scale?
cards.back: Audience sweet spot (scaling): 400K-2M (US); 100K-500K (smaller countries). Large algorithm-leverage audiences: 4-5M up to 40-50M as scaling tiers. (Michigan Method / scaling.)

cards.title: A Winning Foundational Ad Needs Little Babysitting
cards.front: How much daily fiddling does a truly winning ad actually require?
cards.back: A winning foundational ad needs little ongoing optimization. One campaign ran 30 days on just 15 minutes of TOTAL optimization with no new ads — freeing you to SCALE instead of babysit.
cards.example: One-Ad Experiment, 30 days: 3,000 subscribers + 33,000 fans at $100/day, ~$1.00/sub, 15 min total optimization, no new ads.

cards.title: What 'Good' ROI Actually Looks Like
cards.front: Those 23,000% ROI case studies — what should you really expect?
cards.back: Realistic ROI expectation: 100-300% ROI is good. Don't expect outlier case-study numbers. (Wicked Reports re-run hit 23,119% ROI on $53.87 → $12,508 revenue, but that was atypical and partly driven by one bulk buyer.) Calibrate your expectations to the realistic band.
cards.example: Wicked Reports original boosted post: $6,730 revenue on $347 spend = 1,842% ROI (still an outlier).

cards.title: Misreading Audience Insights Demographic %
cards.front: Audience Insights says 'Female 45-54 = 37%' — 37% of what, exactly?
cards.back: Bar-graph numbers are share WITHIN a gender, not of the overall market. 'Female 45-54' at 37% = 37% of 39% = 14.43% of the overall market. Multiply the within-gender % by that gender's overall share to get the true market figure.
cards.example: Female 45-54 at 37% × 39% (overall female share) = 14.43% of the total market.

cards.title: Adapt Rules to Offline / Untrackable Sales
cards.front: Your sales happen in-store with no pixel — do the conversion rules still bind you?
cards.back: Adapt conversion-count rules to offline/untrackable sales. Guidelines are a FRAMEWORK, not a literal requirement. Real-world lift can be invisible to attribution.
cards.example: Home Depot in-store sales spiked with no trackable attribution.

cards.title: Don't Drown in Reporting — Go Section by Section
cards.front: Reporting feels like a wall of numbers — how do you not freeze?
cards.back: Don't get overwhelmed by reporting. Work through each section methodically, section by section, instead of trying to absorb everything at once. Confidence in data-driven decisions builds over time. Simplify rather than absorbing everything at once.
